articles.name: Announcing Amazon S3 as an external content source for use across knowledge experiences
articles.title: Announcing Amazon S3 as an external content source for use across knowledge experiences
articles.body:
We’re excited to announce the general availability of Amazon S3 as a knowledge source. You can now connect Amazon S3 to your Zendesk account to make the content available wherever external content is used.

This announcement includes the following topics:

  • What is changing?
  • Why is Zendesk making this change?
  • What do I need to do?

What is changing?

You can now connect Amazon S3 as an external knowledge source to your Zendesk account to make the content available wherever external content is used. This expands the content sources available, enabling access to your existing Amazon S3 content across your knowledge experiences.

Why is Zendesk making this change?

We’re making it easier to reuse existing knowledge from different sources your organization already uses and leverage it within Zendesk. So now, your AI agents, search, and generative search can tap into your Amazon S3 knowledge.

What do I need to do?

You can create an Amazon S3 connection on any Zendesk Suite or Knowledge plan. For more information, see the following resources:

  • Connecting Amazon S3 as an external knowledge source
  • Connecting knowledge sources to power generative replies in agents

If you have feedback or questions related to this announcement, visit our community forum where we collect and manage customer product feedback.

articles.name: Release notes through 2026-06-19
articles.title: Release notes through 2026-06-19
articles.body:
See the What's New section for an overview of what was released last month.

This week's release notes include:

  • Voice
  • App Marketplace
  • Products with no updates this week

Voice

New:

  • Admins can now configure how many times an IVR greeting repeats (from one to three) before routing to the default option. See Routing incoming calls with IVR.

Fixed:

  • Improvements to the speaker diarization model used to label speakers as "Agent" and "Customer" in post-call transcripts. 

App Marketplace

New:
  • XCHK Verification (Support)
    • XCHK Verification provides real-time identity verification for remote interactions. XCHK Verification lets Zendesk agents launch a live video verification session directly from a ticket sidebar. The app verifies the requester's real-time location against their expected location and checks device authenticity, including detection of browser language/timezone spoofing and VPN/proxy use. After the session, PASS or FAIL results are posted back to the Zendesk ticket as an internal note with tags, and can trigger Zendesk automations. When Zendesk OAuth is connected in XCHK, results sync automatically back to the ticket.
  • Quick Charts (Support) (paid)
    • Quick Charts puts live ticket visualizations one click away. Open the top bar popover, pick a view, and see your queue broken down by status, priority, assignee workload, channel, and ticket type - all without leaving the agent workspace.
  • Macro Hero (Support)
    • Macro Hero is the missing macro management tool for Zendesk admins and CX teams. If you have hundreds (or thousands) of macros, you know how painful it is to keep them organized, up to date, and consistent. Macro Hero brings powerful bulk operations, inline editing, and usage analytics — all in a clean, intuitive interface that lives right in your Zendesk navigation bar.
  • Uniti AI (Support) (paid)
    • Uniti AI handles your customer conversations across voice, SMS, WhatsApp, email, and web chat. This integration captures every one of them as a structured Zendesk ticket — with the full transcript, an AI-generated summary, intent tags, and custom field data — so your support, sales, and marketing teams see the full picture in real time.
  • LeadDesk Talk v2 (Support)
    • LeadDesk Talk v2 helps your agents manage calls and SMS directly in Zendesk. Call and SMS activity is matched to tickets under a customer profile, so you can track the entire ticket history in Zendesk. Make and receive support calls directly in Zendesk.  Send SMS follow-ups with additional support messages.  Calls open new tickets, and messages are recorded as notes.
  • toern returns (Support)
    • toern optimizes returns and exchange requests through intelligent, automated workflows. The solution integrates seamlessly into existing shop and logistics systems, transforming the return process into a positive customer experience. By leveraging AI, toern relieves the burden on support teams and provides valuable data to optimize your business operations. Give full visibility into all return processes directly within Zendesk Support tickets.
  • Data Manager (Support) (paid)
    • Data Manager is an audit and visibility tool for Zendesk admins.  Data Manager gives you a fast, sortable spreadsheet view of your Users, Organizations, and Business Rules — built right into the agent interface.
  • ProductBridge (Support)
    • ProductBridge helps product teams capture, organize, and prioritize customer feedback from Zendesk tickets. The sidebar app lets support agents search existing feedback posts, create new ones, upvote on behalf of customers, and link tickets to posts — all without leaving Zendesk. AI-powered detection surfaces product feedback automatically, and smart deduplication keeps things tidy across all connected sources (Zendesk, Slack, Intercom, Jira, Linear, and more).
  • Onetime Secret (Support)
    • Onetime Secret lets you create encrypted, self-destructing links to share passwords, credentials, and other sensitive data with customers directly from your ticket workspace. Secrets are encrypted at rest, viewable exactly once, then permanently deleted. Supports all Onetime Secret regions (US, EU, CA, UK, NZ) and custom domains for branded sharing links.
  • Super Views (Support) (paid)
    • Super Views replaces native Zendesk ticket views with a faster, richer way to see and triage your tickets. Super Views shows up to 1000 tickets per view with multi-column sorting, color coding, and instant CSV export - all inside the agent workspace.

Products with no updates this week

  • Admin Center
  • AI agents
  • Support
  • AI agents - Advanced
  • Analytics
  • Copilot
  • Knowledge
  • Zendesk Workforce Management (WFM)
  • Zendesk QA

articles.name: Understanding how Contact Center calls become tickets
articles.title: Understanding how Contact Center calls become tickets
articles.body:
When an agent answers or makes a call through the console, Zendesk creates a ticket. The ticket is associated with the calling number in either an existing end-user profile or a new profile that uses the calling number as the direct line. This article describes the circumstances where Zendesk creates and doesn’t create new tickets on calls and how profiles are linked to tickets.

Summary: ◀▼

When you answer or make calls through the console, a ticket is created and linked to the caller's profile. Inbound calls or voicemails generate new tickets unless linked to an open ticket ID. Outbound calls create tickets unless initiated from the ticket composer. Tickets include call details and optionally recordings or transcripts. You can merge new tickets with existing ones if the call relates to an ongoing issue.

When an agent answers or makes a call through the console, Zendesk creates a ticket. The ticket is associated with the calling number in either an existing end-user profile or a new profile that uses the calling number as the direct line. This article describes the circumstances where Zendesk creates and doesn’t create new tickets on calls and how profiles are linked to tickets.

This article contains the following topics:

  • Understanding how calls become tickets
  • Merging tickets when a call is about an existing ticket

Understanding how calls become tickets

On inbound calls, Zendesk creates a new ticket when an agent answers a call or when an end user leaves a voicemail, unless the ticket_id contact attribute is set in the contact flow. When Zendesk receives the ticket_id of an open ticket, the call or voicemail is associated with that ticket. If the ticket_id corresponds to a ticket that doesn't exist or that is closed, then a new ticket is created.

On outbound calls, Zendesk creates a ticket unless the agent initiates the call from the channel switcher in the ticket composer. If the agent initiates a call from the console or from an end-user profile, Zendesk creates a new ticket. For more details, see Making outbound calls in Contact Center.

When a ticket is created, it contains details about the call or voicemail including:

  • Overview
    • Direction: inbound or outbound.
    • From: caller phone number.
    • To: destination phone number.
    • Date: weekday, month and day of month.
  • Caller information
    • Type: phone, mobile, or SIP.
    • Queue name: name of the queue in Amazon Connect.
  • Post call
    • (Optional) Recording consent: whether the caller opted in or out to the recording of the call.

Optionally, depending on the configuration, it can also contain:

  • A recording of the call, which may be redacted.
  • A transcript of the call, which may be redacted.
  • With the Copilot add-on, a summary of the call transcript.

Merging tickets when a call is about an existing ticket

Sometimes, callers might contact you about an issue already covered in an existing ticket. To handle this situation, complete the call and add comments as needed to the new ticket. You can then merge the new ticket with the existing ticket.

The phone number shown at the top of a ticket is the phone number that caused the ticket to be created. When you merge two tickets that both have voice comments, Zendesk shows the number associated with the ticket you merge into. For details, see Merging tickets.

articles.name: What are the WhatsApp moderation decisions in Zendesk?
articles.title: What are the WhatsApp moderation decisions in Zendesk?
articles.body:
What are the WhatsApp moderation decisions in Zendesk?

When you connect your WhatsApp number in Zendesk, Zendesk provides the integration, but WhatsApp (Meta) remains the decision-maker for certain moderation-related actions.

That means Zendesk does not have control over:

  • Display name approval
  • Template categorization
  • Any other WhatsApp moderation or review decisions

If a display name, template, or account-related action is rejected, submit the appeal directly through WhatsApp or Meta business tools. Zendesk cannot override or appeal those decisions on your behalf.

This article contains the following sections:

  • How to manage templates and phone numbers in WhatsApp Business Manager
  • How to appeal other account activity decisions

How to manage templates and phone numbers in WhatsApp Business Manager

To manage your templates and phone numbers:

  1. Sign in to your Meta account and access your company portfolio
  2. Select the WhatsApp Business account you want to manage
  3. Click WhatsApp Manager

How to appeal other account activity decisions

For any other appeal related to your WhatsApp business account activity, go to:

https://business.facebook.com/business-support-home/<businessID>/<assetID>/

To replace:

  • <businessID> with your Business ID
  • <assetID> with the Asset ID for your template or account

You can find these values in the URL of your WhatsApp Business Manager account.

Important: While Zendesk makes it possible to connect and use WhatsApp in the app, WhatsApp or Meta handles all moderation, approval, and appeal decisions. Make any request to review or reverse those decisions through the appropriate WhatsApp Business or Meta Business interface.

articles.name: Announcing configurable IVR greeting repeat count for Zendesk Voice
articles.title: Announcing configurable IVR greeting repeat count for Zendesk Voice
articles.body:
We’re updating Zendesk Voice (Talk) multi-level interactive voice response (IVR) to give admins more control over the caller experience and to modernize the settings interface.

This announcement answers the following questions:

  • What is changing?
  • Why is Zendesk making this change?
  • What do I need to do?

What is changing?

Previously, the IVR greeting repeated three times before the call fell back to the default option. This behavior was hardcoded, which limited flexibility for customers seeking a faster routing flow.

With this update, admins can now configure how many times the IVR greeting repeats, from one to three, before routing to the default option.

To preserve the current experience and avoid any disruptions, the default remains at three.

We’ve also refreshed the IVR settings page to match Zendesk’s modern look and feel for a cleaner, more consistent experience.

Why is Zendesk making this change?

This update gives admins more flexibility to tune IVR flows to their needs. A quicker fallback to the default option will help callers reach the right agent or department faster. If preferred, the existing three-repeat behavior will continue to work as expected.

The updated IVR settings page aligns with Zendesk's latest platform design standards.

What do I need to do?

  • No action is required if you want to keep the current behavior.
  • If you want callers to reach the default option sooner, update the repeat count in your IVR settings. For more information, see Routing incoming calls with IVR.

If you have feedback or questions about this announcement, visit our community forum, where we collect and manage customer product feedback. For general assistance with your Zendesk products, contact Zendesk Customer Support.

articles.name: Service Incident - May 29, 2026 - Support | All Pods - 401 blank page errors in Salesforce Apps
articles.title: Service Incident - May 29, 2026 - Support | All Pods - 401 blank page errors in Salesforce Apps
articles.body:
Summary

Between May 29, 2026, 07:41 UTC and June 1, 2026, 01:54 UTC, customers using Salesforce Zendesk Ticket Viewer and Canvas app across multiple pods experienced blank pages and access errors that prevented viewing Zendesk tickets. Additionally, some customers faced disruptions in synchronizing data between Salesforce and Zendesk.

Timeline

May 29, 2026 12:31 UTC | May 29, 2026 5:31 AM PT

We are aware of 4XX blank page errors occurring on Salesforce apps. While our engineers actively investigate the issue, we recommend the following steps to potentially resolve it: navigate to the Admin Center, select Integrations, disconnect the Salesforce app via the menu, and then reconnect your Salesforce instance. Please provide feedback on whether these steps resolve the issue, and contact us if the problem persists.

May 29, 2026 15:18 UTC | May 29, 2026 8:18 AM PT

Thanks for reporting this. It turns out that Zendesk developers recently had to make a change to the Oauth Token renewal portion of our integration with Salesforce. This change may have impacted the token that would've been used by your Salesforce integration. Could you please try taking the following steps to see if it helps to resolve the issue:

Open a new INCOGNITO browser window - this is to help avoid issues that can arise due to browser caching.

  1. Within that incognito window, navigate to Admin Center > Apps and Integrations > Salesforce
  2. Click into your Salesforce connection that's failing
  3. Click the Actions drop-down and then Disconnect button in the top-right corner of the page:
  4. You can immediately re-add the connection (still within that Incognito window) - all your previous settings will be saved, but you'll need to re-enable the features you want to use by checking the Enable checkbox on those feature pages.
  5. When reconnecting, please be sure to use a user that has the required profile permissions.

May 29, 2026 17:19 UTC | May 29, 2026 10:19 AM PT

We have another update regarding the issues impacting the Zendesk Salesforce integration. We apologize for the continued inconvenience, but we are now investigating an issue that is impacting the Salesforce ticket viewer indicating users do not have permission to view Zendesk tickets.
 
We will provide an update as soon as one is available. Your continued patience is greatly appreciated.

May 29, 2026 18:37 UTC | May 29, 2026 11:37 AM PT

Just an update, our engineers are still actively investigating the issue preventing some user's access to the Salesforce Zendesk Ticket Viewer, accessed within Salesforce.
 
If you are still experiencing issues accessing the Zendesk Salesforce Sidebar APP as seen in Zendesk Support, please proceed with reconnecting your Salesforce integration and ensure that you grant access to all users while doing so.
 
We will provide additional updates and/or instructions to restore full access to the integration platform as soon as possible.

May 29, 2026 21:07 UTC | May 29, 2026 2:07 PM PT

We are continuing our investigation and working with Salesforce to identify the root cause and resolve the issue impacting access to the integration. Thank you for your understanding and patience.

May 30, 2026 00:26 UTC | May 29, 2026 5:26 PM PT

We’re continuing to work closely with Salesforce on this issue and have an active support case open with them, including diagnostic information we’ve gathered. In parallel, we are testing an alternative workaround/fix to restore ticket viewer access for impacted users, and we’ll share another update as soon as it becomes available.
Thank you for your patience as we continue to work to resolve this.

May 30, 2026 3:06 UTC | May 29, 2026 8:06 PM PT

We are glad to update you that the workaround fix has been deployed and enabled for your account. Please validate in your Salesforce integration that users can access the Zendesk ticket viewer again, and let us know your feedback.
 
If you are still seeing the issue after the workaround has been enabled for your account, please disconnect and reconnect the Zendesk–Salesforce integration (this refreshes the connection state/tokens), then test again. 
If it continues to fail after reconnecting, please reply with the affected Salesforce user(s), a timestamp of the most recent failure, and a screenshot of the error so we can investigate further.
 
Thank you for your patience as we continue to work to resolve this.

June 2, 2026 6:30 UTC | June 1, 2026 11:30 PM PT

We continue to observe recovery after the deployment of the fix across affected customers, and we are monitoring closely.
 
If you are still experiencing issues, please disconnect and reconnect the Zendesk–Salesforce integration using the steps in this guide:
https://support.zendesk.com/hc/en-us/articles/4408834115738-Setting-up-ticket-view-in-Salesforce
Please ensure that under OAuth Policies, Permitted Users are set to: Admin approved users are pre-authorized, then click Save. This is a required setting.
Users do not need to re-authenticate with OAuth each time.
 
We also recommend reviewing the Salesforce integration resources guide here and following the setup instructions carefully:
https://support.zendesk.com/hc/en-us/articles/4408827957274-Salesforce-integration-resources
 
After reconnecting and reviewing the guides, kindly please test again.
Thank you for your patience.

June 4, 2026 1:21 UTC | June 3, 2026 6:21 PM PT

We would like to inform you that the issue was not caused by Zendesk or by customer actions. The issue appears to be on the Salesforce side, and we are actively coordinating with them to have it resolved as soon as possible.
 
We sincerely apologize for the inconvenience this has caused and greatly appreciate your understanding.

June 8, 2026 6:29 UTC | June 7, 2026 11:29 PM PT

The Salesforce-side product setting bug has been disabled, fully restoring integration services. However, because the issue invalidated existing tokens, customers who are still experiencing problems with their Salesforce integration — such as Ticket Viewer issues or account sync errors that started on Friday, May 29, 2026 — will need to complete a one-time disconnect and reconnect of the integration.
This step is required to refresh the connection and generate new valid tokens. Please carefully review the setup guides below before reconnecting:
 

  • https://support.zendesk.com/hc/en-us/articles/4408827957274-Salesforce-integration-resources
  • https://support.zendesk.com/hc/en-us/articles/4408834115738-Setting-up-ticket-view-in-Salesforce


Key steps
 

  1. Confirm the Connected App setup forSalesforce Integration for Zendesk matches our documentation, including:
    • OAuth policy set toAdmin approved users are pre-authorized
    • Relevant profiles added underManage Profiles
  2. Revoke OAuth tokens for the Connected App in Salesforce
  3. Note any active integration features for later re-enabling
  4. Disconnect the integration in Zendesk Admin Center
  5. Reconnect the integration using an admin with appropriate permissions
  6. Re-enable the required features from step 3
  7. Test to confirm all features — especially Ticket View — work for all relevant users


If you continue to experience issues after completing these steps, or if you need guidance on the process, please let us know.

June 11, 2026 00:48 UTC | June 10, 2026 5:48 PM PT

The issue affecting access to the Salesforce Zendesk Ticket Viewer within Salesforce has now been resolved.

Root Cause Analysis

The incident was caused by a configuration error on our partner provider's side related to security settings that invalidated login tokens, resulting in widespread access errors and missing content within the provider's ticket view app.

Resolution

To fix the problem, a temporary solution was activated to immediately restore Ticket View access. Our team worked closely with the partner provider to disable the setting that was causing login token problems, confirmed recovery, removed the temporary fix, and supported affected customers with a one-time disconnect and reconnect to refresh tokens.

Remediation Items

  1. Review alert settings with the service reliability team to understand why the issue was not flagged promptly.
  2. Update the internal guidance pages related to the partner/provider integration.
  3. Review and improve partner/provider integration setup documentation to ensure accuracy.
  4. Enhance monitoring for the partner/provider features that were impacted.
  5. Create a clear, updated support and escalation guide for handling partner/provider-related production incidents.
  6. Continue working closely with the partner/provider to identify the root cause and develop a permanent fix.

FOR MORE INFORMATION

For current system status information about Zendesk and specific impacts to your account, visit our system status page. If you have additional questions about this incident, contact Zendesk customer support.

articles.name: Announcing access log AI descriptions for easier review
articles.title: Announcing access log AI descriptions for easier review
articles.body:
Zendesk is adding AI-generated descriptions for individual access log entries to make complex event data easier to interpret at a glance. 

This announcement includes the following topics:

  • What's changing?
  • Why is Zendesk making this change?
  • What do I need to do?

What's changing?

This update is a continuation of recent access log enhancements and responds to customer needs for faster, clearer investigations. Zendesk is introducing an AI-powered, on-demand, detailed log description for individual access log events to help less-technical users quickly understand what happened, why it may matter, and what to check next.

This enhancement is designed to make access logs easier to use for security, compliance, and fraud investigations without requiring admins to manually interpret complex log data.

Updates include:

  • An AI-generated event description: Creates a structured natural-language explanation for a single log event.
  • Simplified language: Translates technical log data into easier-to-understand wording.
  • Investigation guidance: Highlights why an event may be relevant and suggests what to review next.
  • Better usability for non-technical users: Helps make access logs more approachable and actionable.

Why is Zendesk making this change?

Zendesk is improving access logs to make investigations faster and easier for a broader range of users, including those who are less technical. Customers often need to understand individual events quickly, but raw log data can be difficult to interpret.

By adding AI-powered descriptions, Zendesk helps customers:

  • Understand access events more easily
  • Reduce manual effort during investigations
  • Improve security and compliance workflows
  • Act on log data with more confidence

What do I need to do?

No action is required. This enhancement will be activated automatically on the rollout date, provided you have both access logs enabled and admin copilot conversational assistance turned on.

For more information, see Using the access log to monitor agent activity.

If you have feedback or questions related to this announcement, visit our community forum where we collect and manage customer product feedback. For general assistance with your Zendesk products, contact Zendesk Customer Support.

articles.name: Making outbound calls in Contact Center
articles.title: Making outbound calls in Contact Center
articles.body:
You can make outbound calls inContact Centerregardless of yourstatus, be it online, offline, away, transfers only, or any custom status.

Summary: ◀▼

You can make outbound calls regardless of your status, using your profile settings to choose between browser or phone calls. Calls can be made from the call console, existing tickets, or user profiles, with call details added to new or existing tickets. Your routing profile controls the outbound line, and admins can configure additional calling regions if needed.

You can make outbound calls in Contact Center regardless of your status, be it online, offline, away, transfers only, or any custom status.

This article contains the following topics:

  • Considerations for making outbound calls
  • Making an outbound call from the call console
  • Making an outbound call from an existing ticket
  • Making an outbound call from a user profile

Considerations for making outbound calls

When you make an outbound call:

  • Your profile settings determine whether the phone call occurs in the browser or your phone. For more information, see Configuring voice preferences in Contact Center. If you configure your phone, then Zendesk makes a call to your phone before connecting you to the customer.

  • Your Amazon Connect routing profile determines the outbound line. If you want to make an outbound call and need to change the outbound line, contact your admin to set your routing profile accordingly.

  • By default, Amazon Connect supports outbound calling only to specific regions. If needed, ask your admin to configure additional regions.

  • If you make an outbound call without attaching it to an existing ticket, you can always merge the new ticket from the call with an existing ticket. For more information, see Merging tickets.

Making an outbound call from the call console

When you make a call from the call console, the call generates a new ticket.

To make an outbound call from the call console

  1. In Support, click the call console icon () in the top bar.

  2. In the call console, enter the phone number you want to call using the dial pad or your keyboard.

    The outbound line is determined by your routing profile in Amazon Connect.

  3. Click Call when you are ready to initiate the phone call.

    If you're using your own phone, Zendesk will call your number and then dial the recipient after you pick up.

Making an outbound call from an existing ticket

When you make a call from a ticket, by default, the call details are added to that ticket. You can initiate an outbound call from any ticket that isn’t closed, even if it wasn’t created by an incoming call.

To make an outbound call from an existing ticket

  1. In Support, open the ticket you want to use to call the requester.

  2. In the channel switcher menu of the message composer, click Call <number> or Call Enter a number.

  3. In the call console, confirm the number.

    You can then edit the outgoing number.

    The country code of the number will automatically change to match the number you’re calling.

  4. (Optional) Click Clear on Attaching to ticket <number> to create a new ticket for the call.

    This creates a new ticket.

  5. Click Call when you are ready to initiate the phone call.

    If you're using your own phone, Zendesk will call your number and then dial the recipient after you pick up.

Making an outbound call from a user profile

When you make a call from a user profile, the call details are added to a new ticket.

To make an outbound call from a user profile

  1. In Support, open a user's profile.

    See Viewing an end user's profile.

  2. Under Phone, click their number.

  3. Select Call this number.

    The call console opens with the user’s number.

  4. Click Call when you are ready to initiate the phone call.

    If you're using your own phone, Zendesk will call your number and then dial the recipient after you pick up.

articles.name: Release notes through 2026-06-12
articles.title: Release notes through 2026-06-12
articles.body:
See the What's New section for an overview of what was released last month.

This week's release notes include:

  • Admin Center
  • AI agents
  • Copilot
  • Knowledge
  • App Marketplace
  • Products with no updates this week

Admin Center

New

  • There's a new setting for OAuth clients that allows admins to enroll their local OAuth clients to have their token get the default expiration applied. This means customers can take action themselves on their own schedule, ahead of the planned enforced expiration. There'll be a checkbox at the bottom of the Edit page for turning expiration on (Apps and integrations > APIs > OAuth clients > Edit OAuth client). Once turned on, it can't be turned off. For OAuth clients created after April 30, 2026, the checkbox will be disabled as these clients already have expirations set by default.

Fixed:

  • Audit log events for external OAuth clients now include the client name in the Activity column.

AI agents

  • Additional context for procedures. You can now add natural-language context to a generative procedure to give your AI agent behind-the-scenes guidance on how to execute it, such as steering tone, supplying domain knowledge, setting validation rules, or handling edge cases. Context can be added at the procedure, step, or block level, with broader context automatically applying to more specific levels. Available for messaging AI agents. See Adding context to a generative procedure for an AI agent.
  • New RAG agent. We've rolled out a new version of the RAG Agent's question-answering capability in the messaging channel, using a new architecture that dramatically improves safety and accuracy.
  • Voice: tags for tickets. Voice AI agents can now add tags to a ticket with a call. This is important because tags drive routing, reporting, and automations. This lets customers trigger those workflows directly from voice conversations. Just enter one or more tags in the action's tags field, and they'll be applied to the ticket.

Copilot

New:

  • Starting June 11, 2026, we're launching updates for Copilot intelligent triage that lower the effort to get started, improve over time, and expand across Zendesk. From guided onboarding to smarter topic suggestions, this release builds on our recent announcement, moving from "intents" to "topics," and continues our core vision: giving your team the foundation to automate more tickets, power smarter workflows, and scale your support operations with confidence. See Intelligent triage that's easier to start, scale, and manage.

Knowledge

New:

  • Introduction of Google Drive Connector for External Knowledge Sources. Starting June 11, 2026, Knowledge admins can connect shared Google Drives to Zendesk as an external knowledge source. Content will automatically sync every 24 hours (or manually on demand), allowing organizations to leverage their existing Google Docs and Sheets across multiple Zendesk knowledge experiences without manual content duplication. See Connecting Google Drive as an external knowledge source.
  • Introduction of Zendesk Connector for external content. Starting June 9, 2026, customers can connect a second Zendesk account or sandbox to their primary account. All published Help Center articles from the connected brand will be ingested as external content in the destination account. Rollout will begin on June 9 and will be completed on June 19, 2026. See Connecting Zendesk as an external knowledge source.
  • Editable Procedures. Starting 11 June 2026, customers can edit a generative procedure directly in the visual canvas instead of regenerating it. Select any block to update its prompts, messages, conditions, actions, or integrations, switch block types (for example, convert a Link to block into an Escalate to block), and reset or restore changes at any time. Edited blocks are highlighted so you can see what changed. You can edit existing blocks, but cannot yet add, reorder, or delete them. Available across all channels. See Managing generative procedures for AI agents.

App Marketplace

New:
  • PilotFlow PRO (Support) (paid)
    • PilotFlow PRO is a powerful automation engine built for Zendesk teams that require precision, scalability, and full control over their support workflows. Go beyond the limitations of native triggers by creating advanced rule systems that handle complex scenarios in real time. Automatically assign tickets, apply tags, manage followers, and orchestrate workflows using multi-layered conditional logic. 
  • Flexivity AI (Support)
    • Flexivity AI is an AI agent assist for Zendesk that helps your team triage, draft, summarize, and learn from every ticket. Trained on your team's history, with transparent confidence scores and full agent control over every response. Auto-classify incoming tickets using categories learned from your team's ticket history, suggest responses based on similar past tickets and relevant knowledge base articles, with reference links — no manual searching required, and surface key facts, decisions, and current sentiment so any agent can pick up where another left off.
  • Anthem (Theme)
    • Anthem is a dark, rhythmic Zendesk Help Center theme designed for brands that live in the music, audio, and streaming space. A hot-pink-to-gold gradient system pulses across a deep violet base, with waveform-inspired touches, rounded cards, and glow-on-hover interactions that feel like album art rather than a ticket system. Sora headings bring geometric boldness; inter-body text stays out of the way and lets the energy do the talking.
  • Civic (Theme)
    • Civic is an accessible-first Zendesk Help Center theme designed for government agencies, public services, and civic technology organizations. Built in the spirit of the U.S. Web Design System (USWDS), it uses the USWDS blue banner bar, Public Sans typography (the official USWDS typeface), and high-contrast layouts to ensure every constituent can find what they need, regardless of device or ability. Clarity and trust are not optional here, and the design reflects that.
  • Smao (Support)
    • Smao is an AI-powered phone assistant that handles incoming calls and lets you define automated workflows based on call events. Smao turns every call into a structured, actionable process. The smao-Zendesk integration allows you to create Zendesk tickets directly from within smao workflows. When a caller triggers a defined workflow - for example, requesting support - Smao can automatically create a fully populated ticket in Zendesk, including ticket type, priority, tags, and the associated contact.

Products with no updates this week

  • Support
  • AI agents - Advanced
  • Analytics
  • Voice
  • Zendesk Workforce Management (WFM)
  • Zendesk QA

articles.name: Service Incident - June 11, 2026 - AI Agents | All Pods - AIAA: Technical errors in AI agent conversations causing loss of access to AIAA dashboard
articles.title: Service Incident - June 11, 2026 - AI Agents | All Pods - AIAA: Technical errors in AI agent conversations causing loss of access to AIAA dashboard
articles.body:
Summary

On June 11, 2026, between 16:23 UTC and 17:41 UTC, AIR/Premier customers in the US region experienced loss of access to the AI Agents Advanced Dashboard, with some end users encountering errors during AI agent conversations. The issue was caused by backend instability and was resolved after reverting to a previous system version.

Timeline

[EXAMPLE - to be copied from status.zendesk.com timeline]

June 11,2026 17:54 UTC | June 11, 2026 10:54 AM PT

Our engineers have identified the root cause of the issue impacting access to AI Agents dashboard, we have rolled back the offending change and this should both restore access to the dashboard, but also resolve the issue some end-users who experienced intermittent technical error replies during the incident. Our testing and metrics show complete recovery. We apologize for the inconvenience this issue caused.

Root Cause Analysis

The incident was caused by a deployment error that prevented the AI Agents dashboard backend from starting properly due to a missing configuration. This resulted in the service being unable to operate until the change was reversed.

Resolution

To resolve the issue, the deployment of the AI Agents dashboard backend was reverted to the previously stable version across all regions. This action stopped the system instability and restored access to dashboards and normal monitoring.

Remediation Items

  1. Document and clearly communicate that GCP deployments are prioritized first in the release sequence.
  2. Update deployment warnings to include clear guidance on monitoring expectations during and after releases.

FOR MORE INFORMATION

For current system status information about Zendesk and specific impacts to your account, visit our system status page. If you have additional questions about this incident, contact Zendesk customer support.

articles.name: What are the differences between Zendesk’s two Microsoft integrations?
articles.title: What are the differences between Zendesk’s two Microsoft integrations?
articles.body:
Question

What are the main differences between the two Microsoft integrations Zendesk offers, and when should I use each one?

Answer

Zendesk offers two Microsoft integrations that can look similar at first glance, but they are built for different Microsoft experiences and different support workflows:

  • Zendesk Support Assistant for Microsoft Agent 365 focuses on support agents and broader support workflows within Microsoft Teams.
  • Zendesk agent for Microsoft 365 Copilot focuses on users managing their own support requests within Microsoft 365 Copilot in Teams and Outlook.

This article includes the following sections:

  • Microsoft experience
  • Intended workflows and use cases
  • Scope of ticket access
  • Setup and administration differences

Microsoft experience

The two integrations differ in where they work and how users access them.

Zendesk Support Assistant for Microsoft Agent 365

  • Available only in Microsoft Teams
  • Brings the Zendesk agent workspace into Teams
  • Users can work with Zendesk without leaving Teams

Zendesk agent for Microsoft 365 Copilot

  • Available in both Microsoft Teams and Outlook
  • Brings the Zendesk experience into Microsoft 365 Copilot in Teams and Outlook
  • Users can access the Zendesk agent from the Copilot agents list in Teams or Outlook
  • Users can work with Zendesk without leaving Teams or Outlook

Intended workflows and use cases

The two integrations also differ in the type of work they support.

Support Assistant for Microsoft Agent 365

Use the Support Assistant for Microsoft Agent 365 when you want a more complete Zendesk support workflow in Microsoft Teams only.

Users can:

  • Search and manage tickets
  • Create tickets
  • Add public or private comments
  • Browse help center articles
  • Manage IT assets
  • Work with views and macros
  • Look up users and organizations
  • Use plain language requests and slash commands

This integration is the better fit when you want a broader Zendesk support experience inside Teams and want the experience to respect Zendesk role permissions for different user types.

Zendesk agent for Microsoft 365 Copilot

Use the Zendesk agent for Microsoft 365 Copilot when you want Zendesk support capabilities inside Microsoft 365 Copilot in Teams and Outlook.

Users can:

  • Search help center articles
  • Create Zendesk tickets
  • Add public ticket comments
  • View tickets they created
  • Update tickets they created
  • Resolve tickets they created

This integration is the better fit when you want Zendesk support features embedded in Copilot and only need help center search and management of their own support requests.

Scope of ticket access

The two integrations differ in how much access users have to Zendesk content and actions.

Support Assistant for Microsoft Agent 365

  • Uses the signed-in user’s Zendesk permissions
  • Access and available actions depend on the user’s Zendesk role
  • Supports broader agent-style ticket actions

Zendesk agent for Microsoft 365 Copilot

  • Users can only view and modify tickets they created
  • The integration is more limited in scope and is focused on the user’s own support requests

Setup and administration differences

The two integrations also differ in how admins set them up and how users access them.

Zendesk Support Assistant for Microsoft Agent 365

  • Users install the app in Microsoft Teams
  • Users connect their Zendesk account using a /login command
  • The app supports /logout, /help, and /feedback
  • Access is governed by Zendesk permissions
  • The app is designed to be used directly by end users, agents, and admins depending on permissions

Zendesk agent for Microsoft 365 Copilot

  • Admins set up the integration in Zendesk Admin Center
  • Admins connect the Microsoft account
  • Admins can enable or disable individual features
  • A Microsoft 365 admin must also allow users to install and use the Zendesk agent
  • Multiple Microsoft account connections are supported

articles.name: Why doesn’t my webhook payload include the URL behind a link in a ticket comment?
articles.title: Why doesn’t my webhook payload include the URL behind a link in a ticket comment?
articles.body:
Why link URLs aren't in my webhook payload

When the system sends comment placeholders to a webhook or uniform resource locator (URL) target, the system renders the content as unformatted text instead of full HTML. Because of this, the webhook payload won't include hyperlinks that exist only as the underlying href of a button or formatted HTML link.

For example, a ticket comment can contain text or an image with a hyperlink, such as Click here.

When a webhook payload uses a comment placeholder, it includes only the visible text associated with an image or link, not the destination URL behind it. Any image URL that appears in the payload is typically the attachment or hosted image URL stored by the system, not the hyperlink target behind the image or button.

This happens when you use comment data placeholders such as:

  • ticket.description
  • ticket.latest_comment_html
  • ticket.latest_public_comment
  • ticket.latest_public_comment_html

Why does the link still work in the Zendesk UI?

The Zendesk ticket interface can render email or comment content to view, which includes clickable images, buttons, or formatted links. However, webhook placeholders don't return the same content as fully preserved HTML. Instead, they use a processed text version of the comment or description.

As a result, the behavior in the user interface (UI) can differ from the content in the webhook payload.

Why do some links still appear in the payload?

Some links can still appear if they are present in the plain-text version of the email or comment.

For example, a webhook payload can include:

  • a raw URL written directly in the message
  • a link preserved in plain text or Markdown format
  • image or attachment links represented in a text-friendly format

By contrast, links embedded only inside more complex HTML structures, such as table-based buttons, won't be preserved in the webhook payload.

Is there a placeholder that reliably returns the hidden URL?

There's no placeholder that reliably extracts and returns the underlying hyperlink URL for button-style or HTML-formatted links in webhook payloads.

What can I do if I need the exact URL from the ticket comments?

If your workflow requires the actual hyperlink destination, consider one of these approaches:

  • Option 1: Extract the URL and store it in a custom ticket field. Use your own logic to parse the inbound email or comment, extract the URL, and write it into a custom ticket field. You can then use the custom ticket field placeholder to include that custom field in your webhook payload.

Option 2: Use the Ticket Comments API

Do not rely on the webhook payload to contain the link. Instead, send the ticket ID in the webhook payload and have your external system call the Ticket Comments API to retrieve the comment content and parse the URL there.

This is generally the most reliable approach if you need to work with the actual hyperlink destination.

articles.name: Announcing audio playback for the email channel
articles.title: Announcing audio playback for the email channel
articles.body:
We’re excited to announce that our embedded audio player in Agent Workspace is now available for the email channel in addition to messaging conversations. This player allows agents to listen to audio messages without downloading files externally, providing a smoother and faster support experience.

This article includes these sections:

  • What is changing?
  • Why is Zendesk making this change?
  • What do I need to do?

What is changing?

Agents can now listen to customer audio messages attached to email comments right inside their Zendesk interface with a simple play button. This improves workflow efficiency and reduces response time.

Why is Zendesk making this change?

We want to help agents provide faster, more accessible support. Many customers communicate using audio, especially those who rely on voice due to literacy or accessibility needs. This change enhances the ability to serve these customers effectively, improving satisfaction and productivity.

What do I need to do?

No action is required on your part to enable this feature. It will be automatically available in Agent Workspace. Just look for the new play button next to audio messages and enjoy the simpler way to listen and assist. See Playing back audio comments in email and messaging tickets.

If you have feedback or questions related to this announcement, visit our Community forum  where we collect and manage customer product feedback. For general assistance with your Zendesk products, contact Zendesk Customer Support.

articles.name: Error: Some Talk features aren't available right now
articles.title: Error: Some Talk features aren't available right now
articles.body:
Issue symptoms

My agents can make and receive calls but this error appears: Some Talk features aren’t available right now. You can still make and receive calls, and multiple features in Talk don't work as expected:

  • The call generated a ticket, but that ticket doesn't pop up automatically in the Agent Workspace
  • If wrap-up is active, the user gets dropped from the call
  • The pause and transfer buttons aren't visible in the call console
  • Even if the option Allow agents to control call recording is turned on in Admin Center, the recording control button isn't visible in the call console

Resolution steps

This message indicates that Talk encountered poor network conditions and operates in an unstable network mode. This mode prioritizes only essential call functions, so that your agents can continue to take calls from your customers. Once network conditions improve, Unstable Network Mode automatically disengages and full call functionality resumes for your agents.

If your agents encounter Unstable Network Mode often, their browser or computer may not be able to connect to the required Zendesk Talk URLs. If you don’t allow this communication, your agents can only accept and decline calls or hang up.

To resolve this issue:

  1. Contact your network administrator to enable communication with your computer or browser
    • If your company already permits all AWS IP ranges, you don't need to add the required URLs for Talk. If the required URLs or their IP ranges have been added to your company network configuration, ensure that the connections are allowed in your VPN, firewall, and any antivirus software you use.
  2. Refresh your browser tab. The error message may appear due to poor internet connectivity.
  3. If you open multiple browser tabs when you accept or make Talk calls, reduce them to less than 4 to see if that reduces the frequency of the issue

For more information, see:

  • How can I troubleshoot Talk issues?
  • What are the most common Talk error messages?

articles.name: Workflow: Closing a large number of tickets
articles.title: Workflow: Closing a large number of tickets
articles.body:
Use an automation or the application programming interface (API) to close tickets in bulk. This archives the tickets and removes them from your views.

This article covers these sections:

  • Close tickets with the API
  • Close tickets with an automation

Close tickets with the API

Use the API to close a large group of tickets. You can use the Advanced Data Privacy and Protection (ADPP) add-on or the ticket API endpoint.

Create a deletion schedule for advanced data retention policies

With the advanced data retention policies of the ADPP add-on, you can create a deletion schedule based on your ticket's Last Updated date and Brand. After you activate a schedule, it searches for and deletes tickets or end users that meet the conditions.

Use the API to bulk close tickets

Use the ticket API endpoint to find and manage tickets. For more information about the API, see Admin's guide to the Zendesk API.

If you're new to APIs, use Postman to send a request to your Zendesk Support account. For steps to use Postman to make API requests to your Zendesk account, see How do I set up Postman to make API requests to my Zendesk account?.

Create an automation to set ticket status to closed

Use an automation to create a time-based rule that closes your tickets.

To create this automation:

  1. Add a new automation
  2. Name the new automation
  3. Add these conditions under Meet all of the following conditions:
    • Ticket: Hours since update > Greater than > (Specify the time here). If the tickets you want to close have a specific status, use other conditions such as Ticket: Hours since open, Ticket: Hours since pending, and Ticket: Hours since solved.
    • Ticket: Status category > Less than > Closed
  4. Under Perform these actions, select Ticket: Status category: Closed
  5. Click Create automation.
Note: In accounts with custom ticket statuses deactivated, use the condition Ticket: Status instead of Ticket: Status category. For the actions, use the action Ticket: Status | Closed.

articles.name: Can I use the same Support account for customer support and HR or other sensitive private teams?
articles.title: Can I use the same Support account for customer support and HR or other sensitive private teams?
articles.body:
Can I use the same Support account for customer support and HR or other sensitive private teams?

Agents can view all tickets by default unless you configure otherwise. You can use one Support account for both customer-facing agents and internal agents. Consider privacy risks, especially if customer-facing agents submit tickets to internal teams as requesters. Change their role permissions to prevent agents from accessing tickets in certain groups.

  • Restrict agents to groups
  • Pros and cons of a single Support account
  • Pros and cons of separate Support accounts

Restrict agents to groups

On the Enterprise plans, designate a group as private to ensure that agents outside the group cannot access tickets assigned to it.

Agents who do not have access to a ticket in a private group cannot view it or search for it. You can also prevent the agent requester, as an end user, from seeing private comments or agent-only fields on the ticket.

If your plan does not include private groups, add agents to groups and restrict them to tickets in their groups from the custom role or the user profile, depending on your plan level.

For more information on restricting agent ticket access to groups, see Creating private ticket groups and granting agents access.

Role and group restrictions have these limits:

  • If an agent must access tickets across multiple groups, the agent must belong to all necessary groups. When an agent reassigns a ticket outside those groups, the agent loses access to the ticket.
  • If you add an agent as a CC on a ticket, that agent gains access in the Support interface, even if the CC’d agent does not belong to the ticket group.
  • If an agent requests a ticket in an Human Resources (HR) group, the agent can see all ticket fields and internal comments. This can be a problem when the HR agent must add sensitive details about the requester.

Pros of a single Support account

  • One interface to manage all groups and departments
  • Single sign-on (SSO) configuration is easier
  • Some existing business rules, apps, and administrative settings can be reused
  • Centralized reporting for all ticket activity
  • Consolidate internal knowledge within one help center
  • You can enable agents to manage and submit requests from the help center to simulate parts of the end user experience

Cons of a single Support account

  • If you are on the Professional, Growth, or Team plan, all agents can see internal comments and agent-only ticket fields. If an agent submits a ticket to HR, the agent can see all fields and internal comments on their own ticket
  • If you add a restricted agent as a CC or follower on a ticket, that agent gains access to that ticket regardless of group membership
  • All admins have access to all data within the HR account
  • Maintenance of groups and custom role restrictions can become unwieldy
  • Custom roles cannot be nested
  • To let non-HR agents see all tickets except HR tickets, your setup may require multiple groups
  • The first reply time service level agreement (SLA) does not apply to tickets that non-HR agents create

Pros of separate Support accounts

  • Non-HR agents do not have access to agent-only ticket fields and internal comments in the HR account
  • Sensitive data stays private when the HR agent uses an internal note
  • HR and non-HR workflows remain completely separate
  • You have more flexibility for custom role permissions in both accounts, rather than requiring the All within their groups ticket access permission

Cons of separate Support accounts

  • This option requires two accounts
  • Single sign-on (SSO) maintenance can be more complex because your SSO platform must connect to multiple instances. In account A, a user might be an agent, but in account B the same user might be an end user
  • Some integrations may not function in multiple accounts. For example, a Jira integration cannot integrate with two separate accounts
  • End users must use separate help centers to manage their requests and view content. Some training may be required to help users differentiate between the two help centers if they use both accounts

articles.name: What is the difference between a solved ticket and a closed ticket?
articles.title: What is the difference between a solved ticket and a closed ticket?
articles.body:
Question

What is the difference between a solved ticket and a closed ticket?

Answer

Agents can set tickets to Solved after they resolve a customer’s issue or complete their work on the ticket. After a predefined period without any updates, the system automatically moves the ticket to Closed status. The major differences between solved and closed tickets are described in the table below.

By default, Zendesk contains an automation called Close ticket 4 days after status is set to solved that gives your customers four days to respond to a solved ticket before moving the ticket to the closed status. The best practice is to close a ticket after three to five days. However, you can adjust this automation to run anywhere between one hour and 28 days.

In accounts with custom ticket statuses activated, Closed tickets display a custom or default Solved status.

For more information on the other ticket statuses, see these articles:

  • About open vs. pending and on-hold tickets
  • Modifying closed tickets

articles.name: How can I improve the quality of my calls?
articles.title: How can I improve the quality of my calls?
articles.body:
Question

I received a message saying Your connection is unstable while on a phone call. How do I fix the network connection issues?

Answer 

Talk has some requirements that need to be configured from your side. If those are not configured, quality issues may show up.

In general, most quality issues are due to non-configuration of the local network or not allowing real-time traffic. When that happens, you will introduce jitter, latency, or packet loss.

The table below shows the biggest contributors to voice quality issues in any VoIP network.
 

If you experience any of the above symptoms

  • Have your network team or IT team review Talk network requirements.
  • Configure your firewall so all Zendesk IPs are allowed.
  • Once you receive confirmation that the network layer was configured by your technical team, follow the tips outlined in this article: Preparing to use Zendesk Talk.

Zendesk recommends disabling the Wi-Fi connection from your computer and using a hardwired connection. If you don't use a hardwired connection, external factors may affect your calls.

articles.name: Can I set omnichannel routing to auto-open tickets?
articles.title: Can I set omnichannel routing to auto-open tickets?
articles.body:
Question

Can I set omnichannel routing to auto-open tickets in the Agent Workspace, so agents don’t have to accept or open them manually?

Answer

Yes, you can set this for both email and messaging tickets in the Omnichannel routing configuration page.

  • For messaging tickets, select: Auto-accept for an agent
  • For email tickets, select: Automatically open assigned email tickets

This feature isn't supported for voice tickets. These tickets have to be manually accepted by the agent.

For more information, see these articles:

  • Managing your omnichannel routing configuration
  • Turning on automatic conversation acceptance for agents

articles.name: Workflow: How to solve a ticket automatically after a certain amount of time
articles.title: Workflow: How to solve a ticket automatically after a certain amount of time
articles.body:
This article is a tutorial on how to automatically set tickets to solved, if you do not hear back from the user after a few days. It is possible to automate the process of solving tickets with automations.

Note: If you use the messaging channel to communicate with your users, you can also configure notifications and solve tickets automatically through messaging inactivity reminders instead of using this automation workflow.

The workflow includes the steps below.

  • Step 1: Create an automation
  • Step 2: Create a view to ensure tickets are being solved

Step 1: Create an automation

  1. Create a new automation
  2. Under Meet all of the following conditions, add the below conditions:
    • Ticket: Status category | Is | Pending
      Only the tickets marked as pending are checked by the automation
    • Ticket: Hours since update | Greater than | 48
    • Ticket: Hours since status category pending | Greater than | 48
      The Hours since update condition starts counting from the last update in the ticket. The Hours since status category pending condition targets tickets in a default or custom pending status that are in this state for more than two days.
    • Ticket: Tags | Contains none of the following | ticket_solver
      The Ticket: Tags condition guarantees that the automation runs only once in a ticket and it won't be updated again by the same automation
    You can add tags, set users and groups, or target specific tickets to optimize your conditions
  3. Under Perform these actions, add the actions below:
    • Ticket: Add tags | ticket_solver
    • Notifications: User email | (requester and CCs) | Add your custom email subject and body. This action notifies your users of the ticket closure.
    • Ticket: Status category | Solved
  4. Click Create automation

Step 2: Create a view to ensure tickets are being solved

  1. Create a ticket view
  2. Under Tickets must meet all of these conditions to appear in the view, add the below:
    • Tags | Contains at least one of the following | ticket_solver
    • Status category | Is not | New
  3. Click Save
    The view displays tickets that are tagged with ticket_solver. The purpose of the tag is to track the tickets that are solved by the automation in a view.
Note: In accounts with custom ticket statuses deactivated, use the Ticket: Status and Ticket: Hours since pending categories for the automation, and Status for the view condition.

For more details about automations, see the article: About automations and how they work.

articles.name: Can views be refreshed automatically?
articles.title: Can views be refreshed automatically?
articles.body:
Question

Can views be refreshed automatically?

Answer

Views don't automatically refresh in Support however, you can use the options below: 

  • You can utilize third-party apps to automatically refresh your browser session in the Chrome store. Those apps are not supported by Zendesk.
  • Another solution is to click from one view into another, which causes all the views to refresh.
  • You can also use the refresh button to manually refresh views.

When your agents use Play mode, views are refreshed every five minutes to make sure new tickets at the top of the view are addressed quickly.

For more information about views, see these articles:

  • Using views to manage ticket workflow
  • Working with tickets
Tip: If you want to see this feature added to Zendesk, you can add your use case to the product feedback page on this topic. See Automatic Refresh in Views.

articles.name: Workflow: How to exclude specific tickets, users, or groups from customer satisfaction surveys
articles.title: Workflow: How to exclude specific tickets, users, or groups from customer satisfaction surveys
articles.body:
Your customers can provide feedback about their experience in Zendesk Support by rating their solved tickets. If you want to exclude a subset of tickets from receiving satisfaction surveys, customize the automation that delivers the survey and exclude groups, users, or tickets

This article includes:

  • Exclude groups from satisfaction surveys
  • Exclude users or tickets from satisfaction surveys
  • Exclude groups, users, or tickets from satisfaction surveys in messaging

Exclude groups from satisfaction surveys

Exclude tickets within a group by adding a Ticket: Group condition to your CSAT automation. To exclude a group:

  1. In Admin Center, navigate to Objects and rules > Business rules > Automations
  2. Open the Request customer satisfaction rating (system automation) standard automation to edit Under Meet all of the following conditions, add the condition Ticket: Group | Is not | (Group's name)
  3. Select Save

Exclude users or tickets from satisfaction surveys

Add tags to a specific user or ticket, then add the Ticket: Tags condition to your CSAT automation to exclude them from your CSAT automation. To exclude users or tickets:

  1. Add the exclusion tag no_csat to a user or a ticket
  2. In Admin Center, navigate to Objects and rules > Business rules > Automations
  3. Edit the satisfaction automation
  4. Under Meet all of the following conditions, add the condition Ticket: Tags | Contains none of the following | no_csat

Exclude groups, users, or tickets from satisfaction surveys in the messaging and social messaging channels

The messaging and social messaging channels use triggers, instead of automations, to send the satisfaction surveys. To exclude groups, users, or tickets from satisfaction surveys in these channels, apply the same conditions to the messaging triggers that fire the satisfaction surveys

  • For the Web Widget and Mobile SDKs channels, adjust the trigger Request customer satisfaction rating (messaging)
  • For the social messaging channels, adjust the trigger Request customer satisfaction rating (social messaging)

For more information, see About CSAT ratings in messaging

articles.name: How do I edit the automatic response sent to someone who submits a ticket?
articles.title: How do I edit the automatic response sent to someone who submits a ticket?
articles.body:
Question

How do I edit the automatic response sent to someone who submits a ticket?

Answer

Triggers and automations send email notifications in response to tickets. By default, the account includes a standard trigger called Notify requester and CCs of received request and responds to each received ticket with the message below:

Your request ({{ticket.id}}) has been received and is being reviewed by our support staff.

To add additional comments, reply to this email.

To edit the trigger message:

  1. In Admin Center, go to Objects and rules > Business rules > Triggers
  2. Locate and select the trigger titled Notify requester and CCs of received request
  3. Scroll to Actions to find the message body
  4. Apply your changes and select Save

For more information about the standard triggers, see About the Support default triggers.

articles.name: Explore recipe: Reporting on first reply time from the Updates history dataset
articles.title: Explore recipe: Reporting on first reply time from the Updates history dataset
articles.body:
In this Explore recipe, you’ll learn how to use the Updates history dataset to identify the timestamp of the first public agent reply on a ticket, then calculate the time between ticket creation and that first reply.

This can be useful if you want to create a first-reply-time calculation in the Updates history dataset. While the default First reply time metric is already available in the Tickets dataset, this recipe shows how to build a similar result using ticket update data.

This approach is best suited to tickets where agent replies are recorded as public comments in Support. For messaging tickets, the result may not reflect the true first agent message because the ticket can be updated later when the conversation transcript is posted to the ticket. For messaging reporting, use the Messaging dataset first-reply metrics instead.

This article contains the following topics:

  • What you'll need
  • Creating the report

What you'll need

Skill level: Average

Time required: 15 minutes

  • Zendesk Explore Professional or Enterprise
  • Editor or Admin permissions (see Giving agents access to Explore)
  • Ticket data in Zendesk Support
Note: This method calculates first reply time using calendar duration only. It does not support business hours calculations. If you need first reply time based on business hours, use the default first reply time metrics available in the Tickets dataset where possible.

Creating the report

This report returns the agent who performed the first public reply for each ticket in your instance.

To create the custom calculated attribute

  1. In Explore, click the reports () icon.
  2. In the Reports library, click New report.
  3. On the Select a dataset page, click Support > Support - Updates history, then click Start report.
  4. In the Calculations menu (), click Standard calculated attribute.
  5. On the Standard calculated attribute page, give your attribute a name like Agent first reply timestamp.

  6. In the Formula field, enter or paste the following formula:

    IF ([Comment present]=TRUE
AND [Comment public]=TRUE
AND ([Updater role]="Agent" OR [Updater role]="Admin")
AND DATE_FIRST_FIX([Update - Timestamp],[Update ticket ID],[Comment present],[Comment public],[Updater role])=[Update - Timestamp])
THEN [Update - Timestamp]
  7. When you are finished, click Save.

To create the custom calculated metric

  1. In the Calculations menu (), click Standard calculated metric.
  2. On the Standard calculated metric page, give your attribute a name like First Reply Time (Mins).

  3. In the Formula field, enter or paste the following formula:

    DATE_DIFF([Agent first reply timestamp], [Ticket created - Timestamp], "nb_of_minutes")
    Tip: If you want to return the result in hours instead of minutes, replace nb_of_minutes in the DATE_DIFF formula with nb_of_hours.
  4. When you're finished, click Save.

To create the report

  1. In the Metrics panel, click Add.
  2. From the list of metrics, choose Calculated metrics > First Reply Time (Mins), then click Apply.
  3. In the Rows panel, click Add.
  4. From the list of attributes, choose Tickets > Updater name, then click Apply.
  5. Select Result manipulation () > Metric filter. Tick the Remove blank values box, then click Apply.
  6. In the Filters panel, add the Updater role attribute and configure this to show Agents and Admins.
  7. The report will match the following:

articles.name: Announcing a new search side panel
articles.title: Announcing a new search side panel
articles.body:
WWe are moving the search tabs and saved searches to a more efficient search side panel. The move centralizes search tabs and saved searches in a unified experience that we plan to expand with more capabilities in the future.

This article includes these sections:

  • What is changing?
  • Why is Zendesk making this change?
  • What do I need to do?

What is changing?

Saved searches and filtering your Support search results by content record type (tickets, users, and the like) are moving from the top toolbar and tabs, respectively, to a left-side search panel. Functionality for these elements has not changed – just the location.

Why is Zendesk making this change?

We are unifying experiences across Zendesk and this will give a more familiar experience as you may be used to in Views. This also allows us to consolidate functionality in one location for search and gives us more scalability to expand search functionality.

What do I need to do?

No action is required. The change will automatically be reflected in your account and you will be able to use the side panel right away. You can also expand and collapse the side panel to get more visual space for your list of tickets.

If you have feedback or questions related to this announcement, visit our community forum where we collect and manage customer product feedback. For general assistance with your Zendesk products, contact Zendesk Customer Support.

articles.name: Activating agent focus mode in Contact Center
articles.title: Activating agent focus mode in Contact Center
articles.body:
Focus mode allows agents to be online for voice, live chat, and messaging but only serves the agent work from one channel at a time. This article describes how admins activate and deactivate focus mode for Contact Center agents. Focus mode enables admins to set conditional routing based on agent activity by channel.

Summary: ◀▼

Focus mode lets you handle voice, chat, and messaging work one channel at a time, preventing multitasking across channels. When enabled, you receive tasks from only one channel until completed. Admins can activate or deactivate this mode to control how work is routed based on your activity, helping manage workload and focus on one customer interaction at a time.

Focus mode allows agents to be online for voice, live chat, and messaging but only serves the agent work from one channel at a time. This article describes how admins activate and deactivate focus mode for Contact Center agents. Focus mode enables admins to set conditional routing based on agent activity by channel.

This article contains the following topics:

  • Understanding focus mode
  • Activating focus mode

Understanding focus mode

What happens when focus mode is on:

  • Agents can be online for voice, live chat, and messaging.

  • Zendesk assigns work from only one channel at a time.

  • Agents don’t receive work from other channels until they finish or leave the channel by concluding the associated work items.

What happens when focus mode is off:

  • Agents can receive work from multiple channels.

  • Channel assignment is not limited to one channel at a time.

Activating focus mode

Admins can activate or deactivate focus mode for Contact Center agents.

Note: For consistency, set the Agent Workspace focus mode equal to this Contact Center focus mode.

To activate focus mode

  1. In the Zendesk product menu, select Contact center.

  2. Under Routing, click Focus mode.

  3. Activate Focus mode for Contact Center channels.

  4. Click Save.

articles.name: Configuring agent statuses for Contact Center
articles.title: Configuring agent statuses for Contact Center
articles.body:
Zendesk allows agents to change their status, for example, from available to offline and changing the status in Zendesk automatically syncs with the status in Amazon Connect. As an admin, you can configure the custom statuses and the mapping from Zendesk to Amazon Connect according to your requirements.

What's my plan?
Add-on Contact Center

Summary: ◀▼

You can configure custom agent statuses that sync between your system and Amazon Connect, allowing you to control availability for voice and other channels. Default statuses like Online and Offline are fixed, but you can create and map custom statuses to fit your team's workflow. Proper mapping ensures agents receive calls only when their status is set to Available in Amazon Connect.

Zendesk allows agents to change their status, for example, from available to offline and changing the status in Zendesk automatically syncs with the status in Amazon Connect. As an admin, you can configure the custom statuses and the mapping from Zendesk to Amazon Connect according to your requirements.

This article contains the following topics:

  • Understanding agent statuses in Contact Center
  • Giving Contact Center agents access to voice for unified statuses
  • Mapping agent statuses between Zendesk and Amazon Connect

Understanding agent statuses in Contact Center

Zendesk and Amazon Connect have the following default built-in statuses:

  • Zendesk: Online, Offline, Away, and Transfers only. For more information, see About unified agent statuses.

  • Connect: Available and Offline.

The default statuses serve as the core system states needed for routing, availability, and product behavior so you cannot change these statuses. In addition to these, you can create custom agent statuses where you define the availability for each channel.

For more information, see Creating custom unified agent statuses.

When you create a custom agent status the mapping from Zendesk to Amazon Connect is automatically and dynamically configured to match the value you select for the voice channel. For example, if you create a custom agent status for agents who are taking only calls, then you would set email and messaging channels to Offline and voice to Online. The corresponding value in Connect would be Available. If you were to change the voice channel to Offline, then the status would automatically be mapped to Offline in Connect.

If you prefer, you can override this behavior and map the values manually, see Mapping agent statuses between Zendesk and Amazon Connect.

Giving Contact Center agents access to voice for unified statuses

For agents to use unified statuses for Contact Center, you must turn on the Talk channel and configure your agents’ role to not have access to Talk.

To configure the voice channel for Contact Center agents

  1. Make sure the voice channel is activated for your account.

  2. Ensure no agents have access to Talk. See Activating and deactivating access to product areas.

Mapping agent statuses between Zendesk and Amazon Connect

You can manually configure how Zendesk agent statuses map to Connect statuses, except for Online and Offline which are mapped to Available and Offline, respectively.

To map Zendesk agent statuses to Connect

  1. Click the Zendesk product menu in the top bar, then select Contact center.

  2. Under Routing, select Agent status mapping.

  3. Map each Zendesk status to an Amazon Connect status.

    The default, built-in values for Amazon Connect agent status are:

  • Auto (Available): When the voice channel is online, automatically maps the Zendesk status to Available in Connect. This value is dynamic and changes to Auto (Offline), if you change the Zendesk status voice channel to offline.

  • Auto (Offline): When the voice channel is offline, automatically maps the Zendesk status to Available in Connect. This value is dynamic and changes to Auto (Available), if you change the Zendesk status voice channel to online.

  • Available: Maps the Zendesk status to Available in Connect.

  • Offline: Maps the Zendesk status to Offline in Connect.

Note: In Amazon Connect, the only status that routes calls to agents is the Available status. Even if you map a non-Available status to a Zendesk status with voice activated, the agent in that status won’t receive calls.

articles.name: Mapping Amazon Connect attributes to Contact Center ticket fields
articles.title: Mapping Amazon Connect attributes to Contact Center ticket fields
articles.body:
Admins can configure attributes captured on calls to customize and optimize contact center operations. For example, to provide agents with context about the call or user. You configure these attributes by mapping Amazon Connect contact attributes to ticket fields in Zendesk Support through Contact Center.

What's my plan?
Add-on Contact Center

Summary: ◀▼

You can map Amazon Connect contact attributes to ticket fields to provide agents with relevant call context. Create user-defined attributes in contact flows, then link them to custom or existing ticket fields. This setup helps capture key customer interaction details, like order numbers or ticket IDs, directly in tickets, improving support efficiency and personalized service.

Admins can configure attributes captured on calls to customize and optimize contact center operations. For example, to provide agents with context about the call or user. You configure these attributes by mapping Amazon Connect contact attributes to ticket fields in Zendesk Support through Contact Center.

This article contains the following topics:

  • Understanding Contact attributes
  • Mapping Connect attributes through Contact Center

Understanding Contact attributes

Amazon Connect contact attributes are key and value pairs that store information about a customer interaction.
